key_insight: A large autoregressive generative model can directly design catalyst structures conditioned on both discrete labels and continuous targets, making inverse catalyst design more controllable.
engineering_relevance: This approach can reduce expensive trial-and-error screening by generating candidate catalysts already biased toward desired adsorbates, compositions, and binding energies.
method: Pretrained a GPT-style model on 133 million catalyst structures for large-scale structural priors.
Fine-tuned on ~460,000 optimized structures with categorical properties and binding energies for conditional generation.
Used numerical embeddings to handle mixed categorical and continuous conditioning in one autoregressive framework.
evidence_signal: The model achieved 98% structural validity, 95% optimization validity, 93% joint match for adsorbate type and composition, and about a 20% binding-energy match rate with 1.5–4× screening efficiency gains.
watch_item: Binding-energy conditioning remains relatively weak, so practical deployment still needs careful validation of how generated structures translate to real catalytic performance.
paper_title: Toward Controllable Catalyst Inverse Design via Large-Scale Autoregressive Pretraining
